client_context.legal_name: Fictional US Health Plan (Autonomize AI Interview Assignment)
client_context.ai_history: Complex, decentralized IT environment. Partial FHIR adoption. Manual PA processing with fragmented clinical data across multiple systems. Exploring AI-driven automation through Autonomize AI platform.

problem_statement.title: AI-Driven Prior Authorization Automation via Autonomize AI Platform
problem_statement.summary: A large US-based health plan is experiencing high costs and long turnaround times for prior authorization processing due to manual fax review, fragmented clinical data across multiple systems, and poor integration with existing claims and core administration platforms. The goal is to design an integrated solution using Autonomize AI's platform to automate initial intake, clinical review, and determination of PA requests.

current_state.process_description: Prior authorization requests arrive via fax, proprietary web portal, and X12 278 transactions. Clinical reviewers manually gather data from disparate systems (FHIR endpoints and legacy databases), review clinical evidence against coverage criteria, and make determinations. The process is labor-intensive, error-prone, and slow.
tools_in_use: Payer Core System (member enrollment, benefits, contract rules)
Provider Portals / EDI (fax, web portal, X12 278 ingestion)
Clinical Data Sources (EMR-agnostic FHIR stores, legacy databases)
pain_points: Manual review of faxed PA requests — labor-intensive, error-prone
Fragmented clinical data spread across multiple systems (FHIR endpoints and legacy databases)
Poor integration between clinical data sources and claims/core administration platforms
High operational costs for PA processing
Long turnaround times for PA determinations
Difficulty meeting CMS-0057-F compliance timeline (7-day standard, 72-hour expedited)
current_state.annual_cost: Not specified — industry benchmark: $10.97 per manual PA transaction (provider side), $3.41 per payer transaction (2024 CAQH Index)
current_state.volume: Not specified — industry benchmark for top-5 payer: 1-3M PA requests/month

desired_state.description: An integrated AI-driven PA processing system built on Autonomize AI's platform that automates initial intake, clinical review, and determination. Reduces manual effort, improves compliance with CMS-0057-F timelines, and scales across multiple lines of business.
target_improvements: Automated PA request intake from all channels (fax, portal, EDI)
AI-driven clinical review against coverage criteria with human-in-the-loop for complex cases
Standardized clinical data access via FHIR R4 with legacy database bridging
Reduced PA turnaround time to meet CMS-0057-F requirements
Scalability across 20 lines of business with per-LOB configurability

ai_suitability_assessment.rationale: Prior authorization is a textbook AI automation use case: repeatable classification task with structured criteria, high volume, significant manual labor, measurable outcomes, and documented reference implementations (Altais 45% review time reduction, 50% auto-approval). Human-in-the-loop for complex/high-risk cases mitigates autonomous decision risk.
favorable_factors: Repeatable clinical review task with structured coverage criteria
High transaction volume creates strong ROI from automation
Reference data available (clinical guidelines, coverage databases, ICD-10, NPI)
Current manual process has documented inconsistency (54% error reduction possible per Altais)
Industry benchmarks validate automation feasibility (Cohere: 90% auto-approval)
CMS-0057-F regulatory deadline creates urgency and executive sponsorship
Autonomize AI platform purpose-built for this domain with proven deployments
risk_factors: Auto-denial carries regulatory and patient safety risk — requires robust guardrails
Clinical data fragmentation may limit AI accuracy until data integration matures
Model drift in healthcare requires continuous monitoring (clinical guidelines change quarterly)

pain_points: Process inefficiency: Manual fax review is the dominant PA intake channel, requiring human reading, data entry, and routing — highest cost per transaction
Integration gap: Clinical data spread across FHIR endpoints and legacy databases with no unified access layer — reviewers manually query multiple systems
Compliance risk: CMS-0057-F Phase 1 (Jan 2026) requires 7-day standard / 72-hour expedited decisions and specific denial reasons — manual process struggles to meet these timelines consistently
Scalability limit: Current process cannot scale across 20 LOBs without proportional FTE increase — each LOB has slightly different administrative rules requiring manual configuration
Data quality: Unstructured clinical notes, varied document formats (fax images, PDFs, HL7 messages), and inconsistent data standards across provider systems reduce review accuracy
Knowledge loss: Clinical review expertise concentrated in individual reviewers — no systematic capture of decision rationale for training or audit

success_criteria.description: Reduction in average time to complete a PA review from submission to determination
success_criteria.measurement: Median time (minutes) from PA submission to determination, measured monthly

success_criteria.description: Percentage of PA requests resolved without human clinical review
success_criteria.measurement: Percentage of total PA requests auto-determined (approved or pended for info) per month
success_criteria.timeframe: Within 90 days of production deployment, configurable threshold

success_criteria.description: Reduction in errors identified in PA processing (wrong codes, missed criteria, incomplete reviews)
success_criteria.measurement: Error rate per 1000 PA transactions, compared to pre-deployment baseline

success_criteria.description: Percentage of PA determinations meeting CMS-mandated timelines (7-day standard, 72-hour expedited)
success_criteria.measurement: Percentage of determinations completed within regulatory timelines per month

success_criteria.description: Accuracy of AI-assisted PA determinations compared to clinical reviewer consensus
success_criteria.measurement: Agreement rate between AI recommendation and final clinical determination on audited sample

stakeholders.name: Kris Nair
stakeholders.communication_preference: Business value, ROI, go-to-market impact. Non-technical executive lens.

stakeholders.name: Suresh Gopalakrishnan
stakeholders.communication_preference: Payer domain realism, integration depth. 4.5 years Elevance Health experience.

stakeholders.name: Ujjwal Rajbhandari
stakeholders.role: VP Solutions & Delivery Engineering, Autonomize AI
stakeholders.communication_preference: Cloud architecture, scalability, enterprise patterns. Ex-Google Cloud (4.5yr), Ex-Dell (16yr), TOGAF certified.

functional_requirements.title: High-Level System Context Diagram
functional_requirements.description: Provide a clear, high-level system context diagram illustrating the flow of a single PA request from Provider/Payer source system, through Autonomize AI platform, and back to Payer Core System for final determination. Clearly label all major components and integration mechanisms.
acceptance_criteria: Shows PA request flow end-to-end: Provider → Autonomize AI → Payer Core
Labels all major components (systems, services, data stores)
Labels all integration mechanisms (APIs, message queues, file transfer)

functional_requirements.title: Inbound PA Request Ingestion Design
functional_requirements.description: Detail the integration strategy for securely and reliably ingesting varied PA request types (fax, X12 278) and converting them into a structured format for the AI engine.
acceptance_criteria: Covers fax ingestion pathway with OCR/document processing
Addresses security and reliability of ingestion

functional_requirements.title: Clinical Data Access Design
functional_requirements.description: Detail how the AI system will securely access and standardize clinical data needed for review, given data spread across FHIR endpoints and legacy databases. Specify the role of FHIR/HL7.
acceptance_criteria: Describes access to FHIR R4 endpoints
Addresses security boundaries for PHI access

functional_requirements.title: Security and Compliance Risk Mitigation
functional_requirements.description: Identify the top 3 security and compliance risks for the solution. Propose a specific architectural pattern or control to mitigate each risk, ensuring HIPAA adherence.
acceptance_criteria: Each risk has a specific mitigation pattern (not generic advice)
Mitigations are architectural (e.g., tokenization, VPC peering, mutual TLS)

functional_requirements.title: Executive Summary
functional_requirements.description: A non-technical summary of the solution's value proposition and why the proposed architecture best solves the client's problem, targeted at CIO/C-Suite.
acceptance_criteria: Explains why this architecture is the best solution

functional_requirements.title: Implementation Phases Roadmap
functional_requirements.description: Outline the major phases of implementation (Discovery, Architecture Sign-Off, Integration Build, QA/UAT, Go-Live). Focus on architectural decision points.
acceptance_criteria: Covers all major phases with relative sequencing
Phase descriptions focus on deliverables, not calendar dates

functional_requirements.title: LLMOps Architecture for AI Model Monitoring
functional_requirements.description: Design the high-level LLMOps architecture to monitor AI model performance in production. Include drift detection and automated feedback loops.
acceptance_criteria: Distinguishes LLM evaluation from traditional ML monitoring
Addresses guardrails and human feedback integration

functional_requirements.title: Future State Scaling Architecture
functional_requirements.description: Propose an architectural approach (multi-tenant vs. multi-instance) for scaling across 20 LOBs. Justify the model for long-term scalability, configurability, cost, isolation, and complexity.
acceptance_criteria: Proposes and justifies multi-tenant vs. multi-instance decision
Considers cost, isolation, and complexity trade-offs

scalability.current_volume: Not specified — design for top-5 payer scale (1-3M PA requests/month)

security.authorization_model: RBAC with least-privilege, role-based access per LOB

sources.name: Payer Core System
sources.volume: Member enrollment, benefits, contract rules for all covered lives
sources.quality_notes: Structured data, high quality — source of truth for eligibility and benefits

sources.name: Provider Portals / EDI
sources.format: Fax (images), X12 278, proprietary web portal submissions
sources.volume: All inbound PA requests — fax is dominant channel
sources.quality_notes: Mixed quality — faxes are unstructured images, X12 is structured, portal is semi-structured

sources.name: Clinical Data Sources (FHIR)
sources.format: FHIR R4 resources (Patient, Encounter, Observation, DiagnosticReport)
sources.volume: Clinical notes, lab results, patient history
sources.quality_notes: Modern systems with FHIR R4 endpoints — structured and standardized

sources.name: Clinical Data Sources (Legacy)
sources.format: Proprietary database formats, HL7 v2 messages
sources.volume: Historical clinical data from legacy EMR systems
sources.quality_notes: Fragmented, inconsistent formats — requires extraction and normalization

constraints.timeline_note: Assignment specifies 12-week implementation roadmap. Phases provided with relative ordering. Actual timeline dependent on discovery findings and team composition.
technology_restrictions: Autonomize AI platform integrated, not replaced (TD-2)
Azure Service Bus for messaging, Kafka as future scale option (TD-3)
FHIR R4 label only, limited depth (TD-4)
LLM evals + guardrails + human feedback primary (TD-5)
No dollar amounts, no timeline numbers, no FTE counts (TD-7)

in_scope: High-level system architecture for AI-driven PA processing
Integration design for PA request ingestion and clinical data access
Security and compliance risk identification with architectural mitigations
Executive summary of solution value proposition
Implementation phases roadmap with architectural decision points
LLMOps architecture for AI model monitoring and feedback loops
Future state scaling architecture (multi-tenant vs. multi-instance)
Presentation materials (10-12 slides with speaker notes)
Demo prototype using Anthropic PA review skill
out_of_scope: Detailed FHIR resource mapping or Da Vinci IG conformance
Specific dollar cost estimates or FTE counts
Autonomize AI platform internals (no public docs available)
scope_boundaries.justification: Scope aligned to interview assignment requirements (Parts 1-3) with Phase 0 decisions constraining depth and technology choices.

assumptions: The client is a composite representing a top-5 US health plan — not a specific organization
Autonomize AI PA Copilot and Genesis Platform are the designated AI engine
The payer has a mix of modern (FHIR R4) and legacy (proprietary database) clinical data systems
Fax remains a significant PA intake channel alongside digital channels
CMS-0057-F compliance is a regulatory driver (Phase 1: Jan 2026 live, Phase 2: Jan 2027)
Solution deployed in Azure (aligned with Autonomize Azure marketplace presence)
Clinical reviewers retain final determination authority — AI augments, not replaces
Auto-approval is Phase 1; auto-denial requires additional safeguards (Phase 2+)
Budget, timeline specifics, and FTE counts intentionally omitted (TD-7)
Interview panel evaluates architectural thinking, not implementation completeness
